turns.content: Why are African national borders so geometric while European ones follow rivers and mountains?

Many African borders look straight for a few overlapping reasons: 1) Late and rapid partition by external powers: In the late 19th century, European empires divided large areas of Africa in a short time window. When diplomats and colonial offices negotiated spheres of influence quickly—often in European capitals—they relied on simple, mappable lines (meridians/parallels, straight segments between known points) because detailed topographic knowledge was limited and surveying the interior was expensive. 2) Cartographic convenience and technology: Drawing a boundary along a river or mountain ridge requires knowing precisely where the thalweg (deepest channel) is, how the river shifts, and where the watershed divide lies. In many interior regions, accurate maps were sparse. Using a latitude/longitude line or a straight “geodesic” segment between two survey markers was administratively easier. 3) “Effective occupation” and treaty language: Treaties often described boundaries using coordinates, straight lines, or simplified references like “from point A to point B,” sometimes with vague terms like “to the watershed.” Later commissions turned these into lines on the ground; where the treaty was ambiguous, straight lines were a practical interpretation. 4) Different timeline than Europe: Many European borders evolved over centuries through war, dynastic marriage, feudal jurisdictions, and gradual administrative consolidation. Those processes tended to align with defensible terrain (mountain ranges, passes) or clear local features (rivers) because they mattered in pre-modern logistics and military strategy. 5) Not all African borders are straight, and not all European borders are natural: Africa has many boundaries following rivers (Congo River segments, Niger River segments, Senegal River) and watersheds, and Europe has plenty of “artificial” lines too (e.g., parts of the Poland–Germany border were postwar, some internal administrative lines later became borders). If you want, we can look at a few specific borders (e.g., Egypt–Sudan, Namibia–Botswana’s “Caprivi,” or the Gambia–Senegal) and unpack the mapping and treaty history.
Can you explain the Berlin Conference’s role without oversimplifying it?

The Berlin Conference (1884–1885) is often treated as “the meeting where Europe drew Africa’s borders with a ruler,” which is misleading in two ways: it neither drew most specific boundaries nor was it the sole driver of partition. But it did matter structurally. What it actually did, more precisely: 1) Set rules for claims, not a complete map: The conference formalized principles—especially “effective occupation”—which meant a European power’s claim had to be backed by some form of administration, treaties with local authorities (as Europeans defined them), and the ability to police and trade. This encouraged a scramble to establish posts inland and to negotiate bilateral treaties quickly to avoid disputes with other European powers. 2) Internationalized certain river basins: It addressed navigation and trade along the Congo and Niger rivers. That didn’t define all borders, but it elevated rivers as strategic corridors and objects of diplomatic agreement, shaping where influence was asserted. 3) Created a diplomatic environment for bilateral boundary-making: After Berlin, most concrete borders were set by a web of bilateral treaties between colonial powers (Britain–France, Britain–Germany, France–Germany, Portugal–Britain, etc.). Those treaties often referenced lines of latitude/longitude or straight segments because they were legible and enforceable in diplomatic text. 4) Accelerated mapping and boundary commissions: Once spheres of influence were agreed in principle, commissions went to demarcate boundaries on the ground. The practical realities—terrain, local polities, river courses, disease, logistics—often led to adjustments, but the baseline was frequently geometric. So Berlin is best seen as a framework that intensified competition and standardized how claims were justified, which then pushed powers to settle borders quickly and in a coordinate-friendly way.
So were African borders decided mostly in Europe, or were they negotiated locally too?

Both, but with an imbalance in power and recognition. 1) European capitals and colonial offices: Many “high-level” decisions—who gets which basin, which river is the boundary, which parallel is used—were negotiated in Europe. These were often strategic bargains tied to other theaters (North Africa, the Mediterranean, even European politics). 2) Local negotiations that Europeans treated as instruments: On the ground, colonial agents frequently signed treaties with local rulers or authorities. These treaties could be used to claim “effective occupation.” But the terms often reflected asymmetries: translation issues, different understandings of sovereignty, and coercion or inducements. 3) Boundary commissions in situ: When two colonial powers agreed on a boundary, demarcation teams had to place it on the ground. They did negotiate locally in the sense of dealing with communities, arranging passage, hiring guides, and sometimes making small practical deviations. But they rarely accepted local political boundaries as determinative unless it conveniently aligned with colonial goals or reduced conflict. 4) Cases where local polities shaped outcomes: There are examples where strong states or resistance affected borders—Ethiopia’s recognized sovereignty after defeating Italy at Adwa (1896) influenced boundaries; Morocco’s status and later protectorate arrangements shaped North African borders; Liberia’s survival as an independent state also constrained neighboring colonial lines (though Liberia’s borders were still shaped by external pressure and negotiations). So “decided in Europe” captures the diplomatic scaffolding, but “negotiated locally” matters for how lines were realized and occasionally for where compromises emerged.
People say Europeans ignored ethnic groups when drawing borders. Is that accurate?

It’s partly accurate but often stated too bluntly. A more technical way to put it is: colonial borders were typically drawn to manage imperial competition and administrative convenience, with limited, selective, and instrumental attention to existing political and ethnolinguistic landscapes. Key nuances: 1) “Ethnic groups” weren’t the only relevant precolonial units: Many areas were organized around kingdoms, city-states, confederations, tributary systems, and overlapping spheres of influence. Ethnolinguistic identities existed, but political authority often didn’t map neatly onto them. 2) Europeans sometimes used existing polities as building blocks: Where there was a centralized state or recognizable hierarchy, colonial administrators often tried to co-opt it. That could mean preserving some internal boundaries or using them for indirect rule. But those choices varied by empire. 3) When ethnicity was considered, it could be strategic: Colonial powers sometimes invoked “tribal” maps when it helped justify claims or implement administration. In some cases they separated groups to weaken resistance; in others they lumped groups together to reduce administrative cost. 4) The scale problem: Even if a power had wanted to draw borders to match social landscapes, it would have required detailed knowledge and a willingness to accept irregular, hard-to-administer borders—exactly what imperial rivalries and limited mapping discouraged. 5) Over time, borders became institutions: Once borders exist, they shape identities, economies, and politics. So even if lines were initially arbitrary, they can become socially meaningful. So yes, many borders cut across communities, but the story is less “complete ignorance” and more “other priorities dominated, with selective use of local knowledge.”
Why do rivers make “natural” borders in Europe more than Africa, then? Africa has huge rivers too.

Rivers can be convenient borders anywhere, but whether they become borders depends on settlement patterns, state formation, and how rivers function economically and militarily. 1) Rivers as defensive barriers: In parts of Europe, rivers could be significant obstacles for armies, especially before modern bridging and logistics. Holding crossings could be strategically decisive, making rivers attractive as frontier lines. 2) Rivers as administrative lines: If two polities already controlled opposite banks, formalizing that division as a border is straightforward. 3) In Africa, rivers often were arteries, not edges: Many big African rivers served as internal trade routes or lifelines through otherwise difficult terrain. Colonial powers often wanted control of both banks to secure navigation, taxation, and access inland. That incentive can reduce the likelihood of using a river as a boundary. 4) Navigability and internationalization: Some rivers were explicitly internationalized for trade (e.g., Congo and Niger regimes), pushing empires to control key stretches rather than accept them as borders. 5) River dynamics: Rivers meander, shift channels, form islands, and create disputes over the thalweg. If you don’t have intensive surveying capacity, a fixed geometric line might actually seem “cleaner” than a boundary that moves with hydrology. 6) Exceptions: Africa does have many river borders—Senegal–Mauritania (Senegal River), parts of DRC–Republic of Congo (Congo River), Namibia–Zambia (Zambezi section), and others. They just coexist with many straight segments created by coordinate-based treaties.
Can you give examples of borders that are straight because they follow meridians or parallels?

Sure—some prominent examples: 1) Egypt–Sudan: The 22nd parallel north is the official boundary for most of the border. The Hala’ib Triangle dispute is tied to a later administrative boundary that deviated from the 22nd parallel. 2) Algeria–Mali and Algeria–Niger: Large stretches in the Sahara include straight segments that reflect colonial-era delimitation using coordinates and lines between points in sparsely mapped desert. 3) Libya–Chad: The boundary includes long straight segments in the Sahara, influenced by French and Italian colonial agreements and later confirmations. 4) Botswana–Namibia (parts): While the Caprivi Strip’s shape is not a simple parallel/meridian, parts of Botswana’s borders include straight segments set by colonial definitions. 5) Sudan–South Sudan (parts): Not a simple parallel overall, but some segments reflect colonial-era administrative lines and survey decisions that can appear geometric. Outside Africa, similar coordinate borders exist: the US–Canada border along the 49th parallel is a famous parallel-based boundary, showing this is a broader phenomenon of modern cartographic statecraft.
The Caprivi Strip always confused me. Why does Namibia have that narrow “arm”?

The Caprivi Strip (now often called the Zambezi Region) is a classic case of imperial bargaining and river access. 1) German strategic goal: German South West Africa (Namibia) was Germany’s colony. Germany wanted access to the Zambezi River, hoping it could provide a route to the east and potentially connect to German East Africa (roughly modern Tanzania). 2) The Heligoland–Zanzibar Treaty (1890): Britain and Germany negotiated a package deal. Germany received Heligoland (a North Sea island) and recognition of claims in parts of Africa; Britain secured Zanzibar and other interests. As part of this, Germany obtained a corridor to the Zambezi. 3) Geographic reality: The corridor reaches the Zambezi, but the river isn’t a practical transport route from there to the Indian Ocean because of the Victoria Falls and other rapids. So the imagined economic utility was limited. 4) Why it’s so narrow: The corridor was defined to provide access without giving Germany large additional territories that might threaten British interests in the region. A narrow strip was a compromise, legible on a map and easier to describe in treaty terms. It’s a good illustration of how borders can be shaped by distant strategic logic that later seems odd when you look at local geography.
How did surveying actually work when they drew these lines? Like, did they literally take a ruler to a map?

The “ruler on a map” image captures the diplomatic stage more than the fieldwork stage. In practice, boundary-making had multiple phases: 1) Delimitation (on paper): Governments agreed on a boundary in a treaty. This might be a coordinate line (e.g., “the 22nd parallel”) or a line connecting points, or a natural feature (“the watershed between basin X and basin Y”). 2) Cartographic interpretation: Mapmakers translated treaty language into a line on available maps. If maps were poor, the line could be approximate. 3) Demarcation (on the ground): Boundary commissions went into the field to locate the line physically. Techniques included: - Astronomical observations to determine latitude/longitude (using sextants, chronometers). - Triangulation networks: measuring angles between points to build a control network. - Baselines measured with chains or later steel tapes. - Placing beacons, cairns, or pillars at intervals. 4) Adjustments and practical deviations: If the treaty said “straight line from point A to point B,” the commission had to decide what “straight” meant on Earth’s surface (a geodesic) and how to mark it across terrain. Sometimes they used straight segments between pillars rather than a perfectly continuous geodesic. 5) Documentation: They produced boundary protocols, maps, and coordinates for pillars, which later become legal references. So yes, straight lines were often conceived in offices, but implementing them required real surveying—especially where borders were contested or had economic value.
You mentioned “watershed boundaries.” Are those common in Africa?

They exist, but they’re trickier than they sound. 1) What a watershed boundary is: It follows the drainage divide—rain falling on one side flows to one river system, the other side to another system. In mountainous terrain, divides can be clear ridgelines. In flat terrain, divides can be subtle and even seasonally ambiguous. 2) Colonial appeal: Watersheds sound “natural” and politically neutral. Treaty language like “the watershed between the Niger and Congo basins” appears in some agreements. 3) Practical problems in Africa: In many regions, especially savannas and plateaus, divides can be broad, marshy, or hard to pinpoint without detailed hydrological data. That makes demarcation difficult and can lead to disputes. 4) Examples: Some boundaries in Central and East Africa were framed around watershed concepts, though many were later simplified or replaced by more definite lines. In short: watersheds were an attractive idea, but often less operationally clear than a coordinate line or a river thalweg.
Are there European borders that are similarly geometric?

Yes, though Europe’s overall border “texture” often looks less geometric because of long historical evolution and dense natural features. Still, geometric or artificial borders exist: 1) Belgium–Netherlands: Some sections are historical enclaves and straight segments; Baarle is famously complex (not geometric, but artificial in a different way). 2) Borders shaped by partitions and treaties: Many Central/Eastern European borders were redrawn in the 20th century with mixed criteria—ethnolinguistic claims, strategic lines, and administrative convenience. Some segments are straight because they follow old administrative lines or rail/road alignments. 3) Spain–Portugal: Much of it follows rivers, but there are also segments that are not purely “natural,” reflecting historical treaties. 4) Microstate borders: Some are quite artificial (e.g., parts of borders around enclaves). 5) Outside Europe but comparable: The Middle East has many straight borders (e.g., Iraq–Jordan, Saudi–Jordan) from similar diplomatic and cartographic processes, reinforcing that geometry is often a signature of modern treaty-making in sparsely mapped regions.
But why didn’t European powers do more detailed mapping before drawing borders? They had scientific institutions.

They did have institutions, but constraints were real: 1) Time pressure and rivalry: The “Scramble” was competitive. Delaying to map thoroughly risked losing claims to rivals under the logic of effective occupation. 2) Cost and logistics: Mapping vast interiors required expeditions, supplies, trained surveyors, and long timelines. In many regions, disease environments and transport difficulties made it expensive and dangerous. 3) Administrative priorities: Colonial projects often focused first on coasts, trade routes, and extractive nodes rather than comprehensive territorial knowledge. Borders could be defined in broad strokes first, then refined later. 4) Sufficient precision for diplomacy: For diplomatic purposes, a coordinate line can be “precise” even if local geography is unknown. It’s a formal precision rather than a geographic one. 5) Later mapping did occur: Many colonies developed more detailed survey departments over time, but by then the macro-boundaries were already set and had acquired legal status.
How did straight borders affect later conflicts? Is it fair to blame borders for everything?

Borders matter, but monocausal explanations are rarely accurate. 1) Borders as one factor among many: Straight or “arbitrary” borders can contribute to tensions by splitting communities, creating landlocked states, or placing resources on one side. But conflicts also depend on governance, external interventions, economic structures, and historical contingencies. 2) Institutional path dependence: Colonial administrations often created centralized states with extractive economies and uneven development. Borders then became containers for these institutions. 3) Post-independence norms: The Organization of African Unity (OAU) largely endorsed keeping inherited borders (uti possidetis) to avoid endless territorial wars. That reduced interstate border wars but did not eliminate internal conflicts. 4) Resources and border salience: Where borders cut through resource zones (oil, minerals, water), disputes can intensify. But many conflicts are not primarily about borders. So it’s fair to say borders can be destabilizing in some contexts, but it’s not fair to treat them as the singular cause of Africa’s political challenges.
You mentioned uti possidetis. Can you explain that doctrine in this context?

Uti possidetis (more fully, uti possidetis juris) is a legal principle that, at independence, newly formed states inherit the administrative boundaries that existed under the previous sovereign (often colonial powers). The goal is to prevent boundary disputes by freezing borders at a known reference point. In Africa: 1) Why it was adopted: Redrawing borders to match ethnic or historical polities would have been extraordinarily contentious and potentially endless. Many groups are intermingled; historical boundaries overlapped; and populations had moved. 2) OAU position: The 1964 OAU resolution endorsed respecting existing borders. This created a strong norm against changing international boundaries. 3) Consequences: It helped avoid widespread interstate wars over territory, though there have been exceptions (Eritrea–Ethiopia war, Morocco–Western Sahara dispute, and others). It also meant that internal diversity and cross-border communities remained common. 4) Critiques: Some argue it entrenched problematic borders; others argue it was the least bad option given the alternatives.
Going back to rivers—what’s the legal rule for river borders? Middle of the river?

Often, but not always. There are a few common legal doctrines: 1) Thalweg principle: For navigable rivers, many treaties use the thalweg—the line of deepest channel—as the boundary. The rationale is to give both states access to navigation. 2) Median line: For non-navigable rivers, a common rule is the median line (equidistant from banks), sometimes described as the “middle of the river.” 3) Bank-based boundaries: Some borders follow one bank (left or right), meaning one state owns the entire river. This can happen for historical reasons or specific treaty language. 4) River movement: If a river shifts gradually (accretion), the boundary may move with it. If it changes suddenly (avulsion), the boundary often stays where it was. But details depend on treaties and case law. 5) Islands and deltas: These complicate matters; treaties may specify island ownership or use specific channels. So “middle of the river” is a simplification; the exact rule depends on navigability, treaty text, and later interpretations.
Are there famous African disputes caused by river-border ambiguity?

A notable one is the Kasikili/Sedudu Island dispute between Botswana and Namibia on the Chobe River. The question hinged on which channel was the main channel (thalweg). The International Court of Justice (ICJ) ruled in 1999 largely in favor of Botswana, based on hydrological and navigational evidence about the main channel. This shows: - How technical (hydrology, channel depth, flow) river borders can become. - How treaty language like “main channel” can generate disputes when rivers have multiple channels.
Nice. What about the Hala’ib Triangle you mentioned—why is that disputed if the border is the 22nd parallel?

The Hala’ib Triangle dispute stems from two different boundary definitions: 1) Political boundary (1899): Britain (administering Egypt) set the Egypt–Sudan boundary along the 22nd parallel. 2) Administrative boundary (1902): Britain later adjusted administrative control for practical reasons—placing Hala’ib under Sudanese administration and the Bir Tawil area under Egyptian administration—based on tribal affiliations and ease of governance. After independence, Egypt and Sudan emphasized different lines: - Egypt generally emphasizes the 1899 political boundary (22nd parallel), which gives Egypt Hala’ib. - Sudan historically emphasized the 1902 administrative boundary, which gives Sudan Hala’ib. Bir Tawil, interestingly, is claimed by neither in a formal sense because claiming it would undermine the preferred claim to Hala’ib. This creates a rare “terra nullius” style situation in practice (though the legal status is complex).
That’s fascinating. Are straight borders always “bad,” or can they be beneficial?

They’re not inherently bad. Potential advantages: 1) Clarity: Straight or coordinate-defined borders can be easier to describe legally and to demarcate with clear coordinates. 2) Reduced ambiguity: Natural-feature borders can be ambiguous if the feature changes (rivers) or is hard to define precisely (watersheds in flat areas). 3) Administrative simplicity: For taxation, customs, and jurisdiction, a clear line can be easier than a boundary that weaves through terrain. But disadvantages can include: - Cutting across communities and land-use patterns. - Ignoring ecological zones (pastoral routes, seasonal migration). - Creating awkward shapes that complicate connectivity. Outcomes depend less on geometry per se and more on governance, cross-border cooperation, and whether borders are “hard” barriers or managed interfaces.
Could you explain why pastoral routes matter for borders?

In many semi-arid regions, livelihoods depend on seasonal mobility—moving herds to water and grazing as conditions change. Borders that are rigidly enforced can disrupt: 1) Access to water points and dry-season grazing. 2) Traditional migration corridors. 3) Conflict-resolution mechanisms between groups that historically negotiated access. If a border splits a corridor, it can transform a manageable local dispute into an international issue. Conversely, if states coordinate (shared grazing agreements, cross-border markets), the impact can be mitigated. This isn’t unique to Africa—similar issues appear with nomadic or transhumant practices elsewhere—but it can be especially salient in Sahelian contexts.
I’ve heard some borders were drawn along lines of longitude/latitude because of “map projections.” Does projection affect the straightness?

Good point. Projection affects how “straight” a line looks on a flat map, but the underlying boundary concept matters. 1) On the globe vs on a map: A “straight line” on a map might correspond to: - A line of constant latitude (a parallel), which is a small circle on the globe (except the equator). - A line of constant longitude (a meridian), which is a great circle. - A geodesic (shortest path), which appears curved on many projections. 2) Treaty language: If a treaty specifies “the 22nd parallel,” that’s unambiguous on the globe. If it says “a straight line from point A to point B,” you need to interpret whether it means a geodesic or a loxodrome (rhumb line of constant bearing), and how to represent it. 3) Projection artifacts: On Mercator, rhumb lines are straight; great circles are curved (except meridians and the equator). On other projections, the visual changes. So yes, projection affects appearance, but many African borders are truly coordinate-defined, not merely “looking straight” because of projection.
Were rhumb lines ever used in borders, or mostly geodesics and parallels/meridians?

Parallels and meridians are common because they’re simple to define. For “line between two points,” practice varied historically: - In navigation, rhumb lines were practical because they maintain constant bearing. Some colonial-era descriptions might implicitly align with constant bearing or with how lines were drawn on specific charts. - In modern boundary law and surveying, geodesics are often the default for “straight line” on the ellipsoid, unless specified otherwise. For many land boundaries, especially those demarcated by survey pillars, what matters is the sequence of straight segments between fixed points. That can approximate either concept, but legally it becomes “the line connecting these monuments,” which is its own definition. So: you’ll see parallels/meridians frequently, and for point-to-point segments, it’s often formalized as geodesic or monument-to-monument rather than explicitly rhumb line.
How did colonial powers choose which parallel or meridian to use? Why 22nd parallel for Egypt–Sudan?

Choices could be a mix of convenience, existing administrative notions, and bargaining. For Egypt–Sudan specifically: - The 22nd parallel was a convenient latitudinal marker historically associated with the southern limit of Egypt’s administrative reach in some earlier contexts, and it provided a simple, easily described boundary for British administration. More generally, parallels/meridians were chosen because: 1) They were easy to write into treaties. 2) They reduced negotiation complexity (“we take north of X, you take south of X”). 3) They could be set to avoid specific contested nodes (ports, oases) or to allocate them deliberately. Sometimes the chosen line was also influenced by earlier maps that already marked certain latitudes/longitudes as reference lines.
If the interior was poorly mapped, how did they even know where a parallel crossed the ground?

Determining latitude in the field was comparatively feasible with 19th-century astronomical methods. 1) Latitude: Surveyors could measure the angle of the sun at local noon or the altitude of known stars (like Polaris in the Northern Hemisphere) using sextants or theodolites. With timekeeping and tables, they could estimate latitude reasonably well. 2) Longitude: Harder historically because it depends on precise time. Chronometers improved this, and later telegraph-based time signals made it much easier, but in remote interiors longitude could be less accurate. 3) Practical demarcation: A commission might establish a series of points along the intended parallel by repeated astronomical fixes, then place markers. Between markers, they might clear a line or define the boundary as the straight segment connecting them. So even with poor maps, you can “find” a parallel on the ground if you can measure latitude accurately enough.
That implies borders might be off by kilometers sometimes?

Yes, especially in early demarcations. Reasons for offsets: 1) Instrument limitations and environmental conditions (heat haze, refraction). 2) Inaccurate chronometers or ephemeris tables. 3) Difficult terrain preventing ideal observation sites. 4) Cumulative errors in traverses between astronomical fixes. That said: - Many boundaries were later re-surveyed with improved methods. - Even if the “intended” coordinate line was imperfectly marked, the legal boundary often becomes the line of monuments actually placed, because that’s what states recognize and administer. This is why boundary maintenance and joint commissions matter: they keep the operational boundary aligned with legal agreements.
How do modern states manage straight borders in deserts where there’s nothing there?

Several tools: 1) Boundary pillars and coordinates: Even in empty deserts, states place pillars at intervals and record their coordinates. Modern GPS/GNSS makes this far more accurate. 2) Joint boundary commissions: Neighboring states may maintain a commission to inspect and replace damaged markers. 3) Remote sensing: Satellite imagery helps monitor encroachments, track marker locations, and plan patrol routes. 4) Practical tolerance: In truly remote areas, enforcement may be minimal; the border is legally defined but lightly policed, except where there are resources, migration routes, or security concerns. 5) Infrastructure as de facto markers: Roads, fences, or pipelines can create an “operational border zone,” though legally the boundary remains defined by treaty/coordinates.
Are there cases where a straight border was later changed to follow natural features?

Yes, though it’s not the dominant pattern. Borders can be adjusted by treaty for practical reasons: 1) To align with a river channel to reduce confusion for local communities. 2) To resolve disputes where the original delimitation was ambiguous. 3) Land swaps to simplify enclaves or access routes. However, changes are politically sensitive. Because borders are tied to sovereignty, most states prefer small technical adjustments rather than major redrawing. When changes happen, they’re often framed as demarcation clarifications rather than “changing the border,” even if the effect is real.
You said Europe’s borders evolved over centuries. But weren’t there also “straight lines” from colonialism in Europe too, like in Africa?

Europe had imperial boundary-making too, but it played out differently because Europe was densely settled, politically fragmented for longer, and mapped earlier. That said, there are European cases of externally imposed or treaty-defined lines that can be quite artificial: - Post-Napoleonic rearrangements (Congress of Vienna) created borders that reflected balance-of-power logic, not purely natural features. - Post–World War I and II settlements redrew borders with strategic and demographic criteria, sometimes using administrative boundaries or transport lines. - In Southeastern Europe and the Balkans, borders often reflect diplomatic compromises more than natural lines. But because Europe’s terrain is varied and its borders often cut through mountainous and riverine landscapes, even “artificial” borders can end up following features simply because those features are everywhere and because earlier polities already used them.
What about Africa’s internal administrative boundaries—do they also look geometric?

Many do, especially where colonial administrations created provinces and districts using similar logic: easy-to-administer shapes, coordinate lines, or straight segments between towns. But internal boundaries also often follow: - Rivers (for administrative convenience), - Ethnolinguistic distributions as understood by administrators, - Pre-existing chieftaincies or kingdoms when indirect rule was used. After independence, some states reorganized internal boundaries for political and administrative reasons, sometimes increasing geometric patterns (new regions cut for governance) and sometimes adjusting to population distribution. So the “geometry” is not only international borders; it can appear at multiple administrative levels.
I want a clearer mental model. Can you contrast “frontier” vs “boundary” and how that matters here?

Useful distinction: 1) Frontier: A zone of influence with fuzzy edges—think of a broad area where authority is contested or shared, often with overlapping claims. Frontiers are common in pre-modern contexts where states lack the capacity to administer every point. 2) Boundary: A precise line separating sovereign jurisdictions, typically mapped and legally defined. In much of Europe, medieval and early modern polities had frontier zones, but over time these often crystallized into boundaries through state consolidation and mapping. In Africa, many colonial borders jumped directly to the “boundary” concept—legal lines defined by treaty—sometimes without corresponding on-the-ground administrative penetration at first. That can produce a mismatch: a crisp line on a map overlaying a landscape where governance was historically zonal or network-based.
That mismatch seems crucial. Did colonial borders become “real” only after independence?

They became progressively “real” through several stages: 1) Diplomatic reality: Once treaties existed, other powers recognized the line, even if it wasn’t demarcated. 2) Administrative reality: As colonial states expanded inland, they built posts, collected taxes, and enforced rules—making borders operational. 3) Economic reality: Customs posts, labor migration controls, and concession boundaries tied economic life to the border. 4) Social reality: Schools, languages of administration, legal systems, and infrastructure created differences across the border. 5) Post-independence sovereignty: New states inherited and reinforced borders with passports, national institutions, and international law. So independence often intensified borders’ salience, but the process started earlier.
Are there places in Africa where borders do follow mountains clearly?

Yes. Examples include: - Parts of the border between Uganda and the Democratic Republic of Congo along the Rwenzori Mountains area. - Sections involving the Atlas Mountains in North Africa influence boundaries indirectly, though many North African borders also cut across desert. - The Ethiopia–Eritrea boundary involves highland terrain; while not simply “mountain crest,” topography is significant. - The Cameroon–Nigeria border includes mountainous and highland areas (e.g., around the Mandara Mountains), though it also includes river and straight segments. Mountains can be used when they provide a clear divide and are known and mappable; the pattern is just less uniform than in some European contexts.
Could you talk about the Cameroon–Nigeria border? I know there was a dispute around Bakassi.

The Cameroon–Nigeria border is a good example of how colonial treaties, natural features, and postcolonial law intersect. 1) Colonial-era instruments: The boundary was influenced by agreements between Britain and Germany (Germany controlled Kamerun; Britain controlled Nigeria). These agreements used a mix of rivers, lines between points, and references to local features. 2) Bakassi Peninsula: The dispute focused on the oil-rich and strategically located Bakassi area near the Gulf of Guinea. Nigeria and Cameroon both administered parts at different times. 3) ICJ ruling (2002): The International Court of Justice largely relied on colonial-era treaties (notably the 1913 Anglo-German agreement) and subsequent legal principles to award Bakassi to Cameroon. 4) Implementation: The Greentree Agreement (2006) provided a framework for Nigeria’s withdrawal and for protecting residents’ rights. This case highlights that even when borders are rooted in old colonial texts, modern international law tends to treat them as binding starting points.
This makes me think: straight borders are more common where population density is low?

Often, yes—though not universally. Why low-density areas tend to have straighter borders: 1) Fewer settled nodes to accommodate: If there are fewer towns, farms, and entrenched jurisdictions, diplomats feel less pressure to follow local administrative contours. 2) Less detailed mapping: Sparse regions were mapped later and more coarsely. 3) Strategic bargaining: Deserts and sparsely populated zones were sometimes treated as buffers or bargaining chips. But there are exceptions: some low-density areas have borders following rivers (because rivers are the main valuable feature), and some densely populated areas can have straight boundaries if they follow administrative lines or planned divisions.
Are there metrics or ways geographers quantify “straightness” of borders?

Yes, there are quantitative approaches: 1) Sinuosity index: Compare the actual border length to the straight-line distance between its endpoints. Higher ratios indicate more winding borders. 2) Fractal dimension: Some researchers treat borders as fractal-like lines and estimate a fractal dimension; more complex borders have higher dimension. 3) Segment analysis: Count the number of vertices (turning points) per unit length, or compute average angle changes. 4) Feature alignment: Measure what fraction of the border coincides with rivers, ridgelines, roads, or coordinate lines. 5) Shape complexity indices: Borrowed from landscape ecology and political geography to compare boundary complexity across regions. These methods can be used to compare continents statistically, though interpretation needs care: a highly sinuous border might reflect natural features, but also political compromises, enclaves, or historical accidents.
Could we do a concrete example: compare Egypt–Sudan vs France–Spain?

Sure, as a conceptual comparison: Egypt–Sudan: - Dominant definition: 22nd parallel (coordinate-based). - Environment: Large desert expanses with few obvious continuous natural barriers. - Treaty logic: Administrative clarity and diplomatic simplicity. France–Spain: - Dominant definition: Pyrenees watershed and mountain crest logic in many segments, plus some river sections (e.g., Bidasoa River near the Bay of Biscay). - Environment: Strong, continuous mountain chain with clear passes and defensible terrain. - Historical evolution: Border consolidated through centuries of conflict and treaty (notably the Treaty of the Pyrenees, 1659), aligning with a major natural barrier. The key difference is that the Pyrenees offer an obvious macro-feature that is both defensible and mappable, while the Egypt–Sudan desert offers fewer such features, making a parallel attractive.
But deserts have features like oases. Why not draw borders around those?

Oases are important, but they’re discrete points rather than continuous lines. If you try to draw borders to allocate every oasis neatly, you can end up with: - Highly irregular boundaries, - Many potential flashpoints (each oasis becomes a negotiation), - Difficult demarcation in a vast space. Colonial powers sometimes did negotiate specific oases (and caravan routes), but often preferred to settle the macro-line first and then handle specific nodes through local administration or later adjustments. When oases contained strategic value, disputes did arise—especially where the treaty line cut near them or where mapping was uncertain.
Are there African borders that are “weird” in ways not explained by straight lines?

Yes—some are shaped by coastal enclaves, historical trading posts, or negotiated corridors. Examples: 1) The Gambia: A narrow country following the Gambia River inland, reflecting British control of the river corridor surrounded by French Senegal. This is “natural-feature-shaped” but politically unusual. 2) Cabinda (Angola): An exclave separated from the rest of Angola by a strip of the Democratic Republic of Congo. Its shape reflects Portuguese colonial claims and later recognition. 3) Equatorial Guinea: Includes mainland Río Muni and islands (Bioko, Annobón), reflecting colonial maritime holdings. 4) Western Sahara: A disputed territory with borders reflecting Spanish colonial boundaries and later claims. These illustrate that not all odd borders are straight; some reflect historical trade, coastal footholds, and the legacy of maritime empires.
Tell me more about Cabinda. Why is it separated?

Cabinda’s separation is tied to late 19th-century treaties and competing colonial claims: 1) Portuguese presence: Portugal had long-standing coastal footholds and claimed protectorates along parts of the coast north of the Congo River mouth. 2) Berlin-era recognition: Portugal sought international recognition of its claims. Cabinda was treated as distinct in some agreements, with Portugal asserting rights based on treaties with local authorities (as Portugal presented them). 3) Congo Free State/Belgian Congo corridor: To give the Congo Free State (later Belgian Congo) access to the Atlantic, a corridor along the Congo River’s lower reaches and coast was recognized. This created the strip of territory separating Cabinda from the rest of Angola. 4) Postcolonial continuity: At independence, Angola inherited Cabinda as part of its territory, but separatist movements have contested this. So Cabinda is a product of competing colonial access-to-sea logic and treaty outcomes.
Is it true that European borders follow “ethnic lines” more than African borders?

Not consistently. Europe also contains many cross-cutting ethnolinguistic patterns, and many borders cut through them (think of Basques across Spain/France, Kurds across multiple states though not European per se, or historically Germans across Central/Eastern Europe). Differences are more about: 1) Timescale: European nation-states often formed alongside national identity projects over centuries, sometimes with population transfers, assimilation policies, or shifting identities—making borders and “nation” appear more aligned after the fact. 2) Demographic engineering: 20th-century Europe saw forced migrations and border changes that made some states more ethnically homogeneous, which can make borders look more “ethnic” retrospectively. 3) Africa’s diversity and intermixing: Many regions have highly interwoven settlement patterns, making neat “ethnic borders” difficult even in principle. So the claim has a kernel of truth in certain cases but isn’t a reliable generalization.
How did colonial mapmakers represent uncertainty? Like, did they use dotted lines?

Yes. Map conventions often signaled uncertainty: 1) Dotted or dashed boundaries: Used for “approximate” boundaries, spheres of influence, or limits not demarcated on the ground. 2) Color washes: Colonial maps often used color to indicate claims, sometimes overlapping or fading in contested areas. 3) Notes and annotations: “Boundary undefined,” “approximate,” or references to pending commissions. 4) Blank spaces: Early maps sometimes left interiors less detailed, with tentative rivers and settlements. Over time, as surveys improved, dotted lines became solid lines, reflecting the shift from claim to administration.
What about the idea that straight borders are a sign of “modernity”? Is that a fair framing?

It can be, if carefully defined. Straight borders often reflect modern state practices: - Abstract territorial sovereignty, - Coordinate-based mapping, - Treaty law, - Bureaucratic administration. But calling it “modernity” can obscure power dynamics. Straightness is not inherently progressive; it’s a technique. It can be associated with: - Administrative rationalization, - But also with conquest and external imposition. So it’s fair to say straight borders correlate with modern cartographic governance, but the normative framing depends on context.
Are there notable straight borders in Africa that were drawn after independence, not during colonial times?

Most international borders were inherited, but there have been post-independence delimitations and clarifications, sometimes producing straight segments: 1) Newly independent states sometimes formalized previously vague colonial boundaries using coordinates, especially where old treaties were ambiguous. 2) In some cases, boundary agreements between postcolonial states used straight segments to simplify demarcation (particularly in sparsely populated areas). 3) Eritrea–Ethiopia: The boundary commission’s delimitation relied on colonial treaties and maps; while not “newly invented,” the modern legal process produced a precise line, including straight segments in places. 4) Sudan–South Sudan: After South Sudan’s independence, some segments required clarification; while many disputes remain, technical demarcation efforts can create straight-line segments between agreed points. So while the basic geometry is colonial, modern legal processes can reinforce or refine it.
If I were to look at a political map and see a straight border, can I infer it’s colonial?

You can suspect a treaty/coordinate origin, but it’s not definitive. Straight borders can come from: - Colonial partition (common in Africa), - Postwar treaties (common globally), - Planned administrative divisions, - Land purchases or cessions, - Even older agreements if later formalized. You’d want to check: 1) When the boundary was established. 2) Whether it follows a parallel/meridian. 3) Whether it crosses terrain that would otherwise suggest a natural boundary. 4) Historical documents or boundary commission records. So a straight line is a clue, not proof.
Let’s talk about the Sahara specifically. Why does it have so many straight segments?

The Sahara combines several factors that encourage geometric delimitation: 1) Sparse settlement and fewer “obvious” continuous natural barriers: Aside from mountain massifs (Ahaggar, Tibesti) and some wadis, much of the Sahara is open desert. 2) Mapping challenges: Historically, detailed mapping was limited; caravan routes and oases were known, but not always precisely located in European cartographic systems. 3) Strategic bargaining: Colonial powers often treated Saharan space as strategic depth, buffer zones, or corridors to connect coastal holdings with interior ones. 4) Administrative cost: Drawing and demarcating intricate borders across thousands of kilometers of desert would have been costly with limited perceived benefit. 5) Later resource significance: When oil, gas, and minerals became important, these straight lines gained higher stakes, sometimes leading to disputes or need for precise demarcation. So the Sahara’s geometry is a product of both environmental and geopolitical conditions.
Were there any African borders drawn to deliberately split rivals or prevent unified resistance?

Intent is hard to prove universally, but there are documented cases where colonial strategy involved “divide and rule,” and boundary decisions could serve that. Mechanisms: 1) Partitioning spheres to prevent any single colonial rival from controlling too much contiguous territory (balance-of-power). 2) Splitting or reassigning administrative units to weaken resistance networks. 3) Using boundaries to separate coastal access from interior polities. However, many splits were side effects of broader bargaining rather than targeted at specific communities. Also, colonial administrators sometimes preferred to keep certain groups within one administrative unit for ease of indirect rule. So: strategic fragmentation existed, but it’s not always accurate to claim every border was designed primarily to split particular groups.
You’ve talked a lot about colonial powers. What about Ottoman or Arab influences in North Africa—did they shape borders too?

Yes, especially in North Africa, where precolonial and imperial structures mattered. 1) Ottoman administrative zones: The Ottoman Empire had varying degrees of control across North Africa (e.g., in Libya, Tunisia, Algeria). Boundaries between provinces were often not sharply defined in modern terms, sometimes more like zones of influence. 2) Sultanates and local polities: Morocco, for example, had its own sovereignty and frontier dynamics, with influence varying by region. 3) Colonial overlay: When European powers colonized or established protectorates, they often transformed these older frontier zones into fixed boundaries, sometimes referencing earlier claims but formalizing them with modern cartography. So North African borders reflect a layered history: precolonial frontier zones, Ottoman-era administration, and then European colonial delimitation.
Is Western Sahara’s boundary straight because Spain drew it?

Largely, yes. Western Sahara’s borders reflect Spanish colonial delimitation in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, negotiated with France (which controlled neighboring territories). The boundaries include straight segments and coordinate-based lines typical of that era’s colonial diplomacy. But the current geopolitical situation is about decolonization, self-determination, and competing claims (notably Morocco’s claim and the Polisario Front’s position), plus international legal and diplomatic processes. So the straightness reflects colonial origin; the dispute reflects postcolonial politics.
How do cartographers decide where to place border lines on small-scale maps when the real boundary is complex?

Map generalization is key: 1) Scale: At small scales (world maps), cartographers simplify borders because the line thickness can exceed the real-world complexity. 2) Generalization algorithms: They may use techniques like Douglas–Peucker simplification to reduce vertices while preserving overall shape. 3) Prioritization: They keep major bends and omit minor meanders. 4) Data sources: They rely on authoritative boundary datasets (e.g., national mapping agencies, UN datasets, or commercial sources). Different datasets can disagree, especially in disputed areas. 5) Disputed borders: Cartographers use symbology (dashed lines, shading) or omit a definitive line depending on editorial policy. So the “straightness” you perceive can partly be a function of map scale, though many African borders remain straight even at large scales.
Could the perception also come from Mercator maps used in classrooms?

Yes. Mercator exaggerates high-latitude areas and preserves angles, which can affect how boundaries look relative to landmasses. But for “straightness,” Mercator’s biggest effect is on geodesics vs rhumb lines and on visual emphasis. Classroom maps also tend to: - Use thick border strokes, - Simplify coastlines and boundaries, - Highlight political borders strongly. These design choices can make straight segments stand out. Still, the underlying reality—many coordinate-defined borders—remains.
If I zoom into Google Maps, some borders are shown as perfectly straight even in rugged terrain. Is that accurate?

It can be accurate if the legal boundary is defined as a straight segment between coordinates or monuments, even if the terrain is rugged. But digital maps may also: 1) Use simplified boundary data for performance, especially at certain zoom levels. 2) Show a generalized line that becomes more detailed as you zoom in. 3) Reflect one dataset’s interpretation, which might differ from another’s, especially in disputed zones. For precise work, you’d consult: - Official treaty coordinates, - National cadastral or boundary commission data, - Specialized GIS boundary datasets with metadata.
Can you recommend datasets or references for studying African borders technically?

A few commonly used resources: 1) International boundary datasets: - GADM (Global Administrative Areas): Good for administrative boundaries; not always ideal for legal international boundaries. - Natural Earth: Great for cartography; generalized. - OpenStreetMap: Detailed, community-maintained; variable authority. - UN Geospatial Information Section: UN maps and boundary representations (careful with disputes). 2) Legal/historical references: - Treaties and boundary commission reports (often in national archives). - ICJ case documents for disputed boundaries (publicly accessible). - Scholarly works in political geography and African history focusing on boundary-making. 3) GIS techniques: - Use multi-scale datasets and compare. - Check metadata about source and date. - Treat “international boundary” lines as political claims, not always universally agreed facts. If you tell me whether you’re more interested in legal history or GIS analysis, I can narrow the list.
Let’s do GIS analysis. How would you quantify how “geometric” Africa’s borders are compared to Europe’s?

One workable GIS workflow: 1) Get comparable boundary polylines: - Use a consistent dataset for both continents (e.g., Natural Earth at a chosen scale for comparability), or a higher-resolution dataset if you can handle processing. 2) Compute straightness metrics: - Sinuosity: For each border segment between vertices or between country tripoints, compute length / chord length. - Vertex density: Number of vertices per 100 km. - Angle change: Sum of absolute turning angles per km. 3) Detect coordinate-aligned segments: - Identify segments with azimuth near 0°, 90°, 180°, 270° (meridian/parallel-like) within a tolerance (e.g., ±2°). - Compute fraction of total border length that is near cardinal directions. 4) Feature alignment: - Overlay borders with hydrography (rivers) and ridgelines/watersheds if available. - Compute percentage of border coincident with rivers (buffer-based intersection). 5) Compare distributions: - Produce histograms of sinuosity and cardinal-alignment fractions for Africa vs Europe. - Use statistical tests (e.g., Mann–Whitney U) to compare medians. 6) Control for scale: - Repeat at multiple resolutions to see how generalization affects results. This won’t fully capture historical causation, but it gives a measurable contrast.
That’s good. But what about coastlines? Some borders end at coasts, and coasts are irregular. Does that skew results?

Yes, so you’d want to isolate land borders. Approach: 1) Clip to land borders only: Many datasets separate international boundaries from coastlines; if not, you can subtract coastline segments by identifying where the boundary coincides with the coastline or using topology rules. 2) Exclude maritime boundaries: Focus on terrestrial borders; maritime boundaries follow different legal regimes (equidistance, median lines, etc.). 3) Segment by environment: Optionally classify border segments by biome or terrain ruggedness to see if deserts correlate with more cardinal alignment. This improves comparability and keeps the analysis focused on the “straight border” phenomenon.
Speaking of maritime boundaries, do those tend to be geometric too?

Often, yes—maritime boundaries are frequently geometric because of the legal frameworks: 1) Equidistance/median lines: Boundaries may be constructed as loci of points equidistant from two coasts. 2) Azimuthal segments: Agreements might specify straight geodesic segments connecting coordinate points. 3) Special circumstances: Islands, concave coasts, and historic rights can alter lines, but the resulting boundaries are still usually defined by coordinates. So maritime boundaries can be very “geometric,” though they’re less visible on common political maps.
Back to Africa: are there regions where borders are less straight overall?

Yes. Regions with dense hydrography, forests, and complex settlement patterns often have less straight borders, for example: 1) The Great Lakes region: Borders often involve lakes and rivers (Lake Victoria, Lake Tanganyika, etc.), plus complex colonial legacies. 2) West Africa’s coastal belt: More states packed into a smaller area, with historical trading posts and varied colonial administrations; borders can be more irregular. 3) Parts of Central Africa: The Congo basin includes river-based borders and boundaries shaped by concession politics and navigation considerations. In contrast, Sahel/Sahara and parts of southern Africa have more long straight segments.
Do lakes make better borders than rivers?

They can be simpler in some ways, but they have their own complexities. Advantages: - Lakes don’t “move” like rivers (shorelines change but less dramatically). - The boundary can be defined as a median line across open water. Complexities: - Islands: ownership can be contentious. - Lake level changes can affect shorelines and access. - Navigation and fishing rights require agreements. - If the treaty says “the boundary runs through the lake,” you still need to define the exact line. So lakes can be stable reference features, but legal precision is still needed.
How did colonial powers handle tripoints where three colonies met? Are those usually precise?

Tripoints (tri-junctions) can be surprisingly complicated. 1) Treaty stage: Sometimes tripoints were vaguely defined (“where the borders of A, B, and C meet”), assuming later demarcation would settle it. 2) Demarcation: Joint commissions might place a tripoint marker if all three parties agreed. If not, they might leave it undefined pending further negotiation. 3) Precision varies: In remote areas, tripoints could be approximate for decades. Modern surveying can pin them down, but only if political agreement exists. 4) Legal status: A tripoint’s precise coordinates may require a trilateral agreement; bilateral treaties can’t fully settle it if a third party is involved. So tripoints are a good place to study the interface between law, diplomacy, and geodesy.
I’m curious about enclaves. Are there African enclaves like in Europe?

There are fewer classic enclaves/exclaves in Africa than in parts of Europe or South Asia, but they exist. Examples and quasi-examples: 1) Cabinda (Angola): Exclave separated by DRC territory. 2) The Gambia: Not an enclave (it has a coast), but it’s an “enveloped” river corridor largely surrounded by Senegal. 3) Lesotho: An enclave within South Africa (fully surrounded). 4) The Spanish enclaves Ceuta and Melilla in North Africa: Not African states, but geographically in Africa and politically part of Spain. Enclaves often arise from historical treaties, trading posts, or dynastic holdings—more characteristic of older European political geography—whereas many African borders were drawn at a later stage that tended to avoid creating many tiny enclaves (though it created other oddities).
Lesotho is a good example. Why is it an enclave?

Lesotho’s enclave status stems from 19th-century regional politics: 1) Basotho polity formation: Under Moshoeshoe I, the Basotho consolidated in the highlands. 2) British protectorate: Lesotho (Basutoland) became a British protectorate, partly to protect it from expansion by Boer republics and later the Union of South Africa. 3) Surrounding territory: As South Africa consolidated, Basutoland remained a separate British-administered territory, and at independence it became Lesotho, fully surrounded by South Africa. Here, the enclave is not a product of geometric colonial partition so much as the preservation of a distinct polity under British protection amid regional consolidation.
That makes it seem like African borders sometimes preserved precolonial states.

Yes, in some cases: - Ethiopia maintained sovereignty (with important exceptions and episodes of occupation). - Morocco retained a form of sovereignty before protectorate arrangements. - Lesotho and Eswatini (Swaziland) survived as distinct entities through protectorate status. - Liberia existed as an independent republic (though under heavy external influence). So the “Africa was entirely carved up into arbitrary units” narrative misses these survivals and the ways some borders reflect precolonial or early-modern polities.
What about Somalia’s borders? Those seem connected to colonial partitions too.

Somalia is a strong example of how colonial partition intersected with a relatively widespread ethno-linguistic identity. 1) Partition: Somali-inhabited territories were divided among multiple administrations: Italian Somaliland, British Somaliland, French Somaliland (Djibouti), Ethiopian Ogaden, and Northern Frontier District in Kenya. 2) Post-independence irredentism: The idea of “Greater Somalia” influenced politics and conflict, including the Ogaden War (1977–1978) between Somalia and Ethiopia. 3) Border salience: Here, borders cut across a relatively cohesive linguistic-cultural area, making the mismatch particularly visible. It shows that even where a strong shared identity exists, colonial borders can distribute populations across several states, with long-term geopolitical consequences.
So would you say Africa’s straight borders are mostly about colonialism, while Europe’s “natural” borders are mostly about geography?

I’d phrase it as: - Africa’s prevalence of straight segments is strongly associated with late-19th/early-20th-century colonial treaty-making under mapping constraints and imperial rivalry, often in low-density regions where coordinate lines were convenient. - Europe’s borders more often reflect long-term political evolution in a geography dense with rivers and mountains, where natural features frequently became strategic frontiers and administrative edges. But it’s not a clean binary: - Africa has many feature-following borders. - Europe has many artificial borders. - The drivers are historical and institutional as much as physical geography.
Can we talk about the role of railways and roads? Did they ever become borders?

Yes, transport infrastructure can influence borders, though it’s more common for borders to shape infrastructure than vice versa. 1) Railways as bargaining chips: Colonial powers negotiated rail corridors and access rights. Sometimes borders were drawn to include a planned rail route within one colony. 2) Roads as convenient demarcation: In some cases, a road or a straight cleared path became the practical demarcation line, especially if it was easier to maintain than a line through dense vegetation. 3) Postcolonial adjustments: Occasionally, states might agree to use a road as a boundary for clarity—though this can be unpopular if it splits communities. 4) Border towns: Infrastructure creates nodes (customs posts, markets) that can become politically significant, reinforcing the border’s presence. So while not as common as rivers or coordinates, infrastructure can become part of boundary logic.
If I were teaching this, what would be a good way to avoid deterministic explanations?

A good teaching frame is a layered causation model: 1) Physical geography: Rivers, mountains, deserts influence what’s “available” as a boundary feature and how costly demarcation is. 2) Historical timing: When borders were formalized matters. Earlier state formation tends to produce borders shaped by incremental conflict and compromise; later formalization tends to produce treaty/coordinate lines. 3) Political economy: What resources and trade routes mattered at the time? Who wanted coast access, river control, or buffer zones? 4) Cartographic capacity: Surveying technology and map quality shape what can be negotiated precisely. 5) Legal/institutional path dependence: Once a border is recognized, it becomes sticky, reinforced by international law and state practice. This avoids “geography did it” or “colonialism did it” as sole explanations and shows how they interact.
Could you add a bit about how “straight borders” interact with modern cross-border regions like ECOWAS?

Regional integration can soften the practical significance of borders, straight or not. In ECOWAS (West Africa): 1) Free movement protocols: In principle, citizens can move across borders more easily than in many other regions, though implementation varies. 2) Cross-border markets: Many communities operate transnationally, especially in border towns. 3) Infrastructure corridors: Regional projects (highways, power pools) can reduce the border’s barrier effect. 4) Security coordination: Joint patrols and intelligence sharing can occur, especially where militant groups exploit borderlands. If borders are “soft,” the problems caused by arbitrary lines can be mitigated. If borders harden due to security concerns, the same lines become more disruptive.
How does the concept of “borderlands” change the discussion?

Borderlands shift attention from the line to the zone: 1) Lived reality: People in borderlands often have kinship, trade, and linguistic ties across the border, regardless of the line’s origin. 2) Governance gradient: State presence can be weaker in borderlands, creating spaces of negotiation, informal economies, and sometimes insecurity. 3) Hybrid identities: Borderlanders may navigate multiple legal and cultural systems. 4) Policy implications: Effective border management often means supporting cross-border cooperation (markets, health, pastoral corridors) rather than only focusing on enforcement. So even if a border is straight and externally imposed, the borderland can develop its own social and economic logic.
All right. One last technical question: when a border is defined by a parallel, is it exactly that latitude on WGS84, or could it be based on older datums?

Excellent technical point. Many older boundaries were defined before modern global datums like WGS84. 1) Historical datum: A treaty might specify “the 22nd parallel,” but the practical realization could have been surveyed using an older reference ellipsoid/datum, or even astronomical latitude (which is affected by local gravity anomalies). 2) Monumented boundary: If pillars were placed based on those observations, the legal boundary may be the line through the monuments, not a mathematically perfect WGS84 parallel. 3) Modern reinterpretation: When states modernize boundary coordinates, they may transform old coordinates into WGS84, but transformations can introduce small shifts. 4) Legal hierarchy: Often, treaties and demarcation protocols specify whether coordinates or monuments control in case of discrepancy. So the “same latitude” can differ slightly depending on datum and measurement method. For precise GIS work, you’d want the boundary’s legal coordinate system and transformation parameters, not just WGS84 assumptions.
That’s exactly the kind of nuance I wanted. If you had to summarize the core reason in one sentence, what would it be?

Many African borders are straight because they were rapidly formalized in the late 19th–early 20th century through treaty-based, coordinate-friendly colonial diplomacy in regions where detailed mapping and locally grounded boundary evolution were limited, whereas many European borders crystallized earlier through long, terrain-sensitive state formation.
Good. Now I want to go deeper: can you walk through a hypothetical treaty negotiation and show why a straight line is the “equilibrium” outcome?

Imagine two colonial powers, A and B, negotiating a boundary in a sparsely mapped interior plateau between two coastal footholds they already control. Assumptions: - Both want to avoid war with each other (costly). - Both have limited information about interior resources and polities. - Both want a defensible, administrable, and legible claim that third parties will recognize. - Both face time pressure to demonstrate “effective occupation.” Negotiation steps: 1) Identify anchor points: They agree on two known points: a river confluence near the coast that both can locate, and a known oasis or mission station inland. 2) Bargain over allocation: They have competing preferences but roughly comparable bargaining power. They need a line that splits the space in an acceptable way. 3) Choose a boundary rule: Options: - Follow a river: requires knowing which river branches where, and how it behaves. - Follow a ridge/watershed: requires topographic knowledge they lack. - Follow “tribal boundaries”: requires ethnographic knowledge and would be contested and fluid. - Use a coordinate line or straight segment: easy to specify and to defend diplomatically. 4) Minimize future dispute: A and B anticipate that ambiguous language leads to later conflict. “The boundary is the 10th parallel from longitude X to longitude Y” is hard to argue about on paper, even if demarcation is later challenging. 5) External recognition: Other powers will more readily recognize a claim with explicit coordinates than one based on vague local descriptions. 6) Implementation costs: They can send a boundary commission later. Meanwhile, each can establish posts on their side without constant arguments about whether they crossed an unclear line. Given these incentives, a straight line (or parallel/meridian) becomes a Nash-like equilibrium: it reduces bargaining complexity and legal ambiguity under information scarcity and time pressure. The “equilibrium” is not morally optimal—just strategically stable given the constraints.
That makes sense. But wouldn’t each side worry that a coordinate line might accidentally give away a resource they don’t know exists?

They would, and that uncertainty cuts both ways. In an information-poor environment: - If neither side knows where resources are, a simple split can be acceptable because the expected value is symmetric. - If one side suspects resources in a particular zone, they may push for a boundary that captures it—perhaps by anchoring the line to a known feature or by insisting on a specific meridian that swings the line. This is why: 1) Some treaties include oddly angled segments: they’re often “resource-aware” or “route-aware” adjustments. 2) Rivers and coasts matter: known valuable corridors (navigable rivers, harbors) were more likely to be contested and thus more carefully allocated. 3) Later disputes arise when resources are discovered after delimitation: oil, gas, minerals can make an old “simple” line suddenly contentious. So straight lines are common under uncertainty, but where high-value information exists, borders can become more intricate.
Can you connect this to game theory more formally?

A simplified framing: - Two players (A, B) divide a territory T. - Utility includes expected resource value, administrative cost, and conflict risk. - Information is incomplete about spatial distribution of resources and local resistance. - There’s a cost for negotiation complexity and a cost for ambiguous boundaries (future disputes). In such a model, a “simple rule” boundary (e.g., coordinate line) can be a focal point (Schelling point): it’s salient, easy to communicate, and reduces bargaining friction. When both players prefer a quick agreement to a protracted conflict, focal-point solutions have higher probability. Add a third-party recognition component (other powers accept claims that are legible), and the payoff for coordinate-defined borders increases. Of course, real history includes asymmetries in power, strategic deception, and domestic politics, but the focal-point logic helps explain why geometry is a common outcome under time pressure and incomplete information.
That’s pretty technical already. I think this covers it. Could you end by listing a few misconceptions and corrections?

Misconceptions and corrections: 1) Misconception: “The Berlin Conference drew all African borders.” Correction: Berlin set rules and a diplomatic framework; most specific borders were set later through bilateral treaties and on-the-ground commissions. 2) Misconception: “Africans had no political boundaries before colonialism.” Correction: Many polities had boundaries or frontier zones, though often not mapped as precise lines; sovereignty could be layered and overlapping. 3) Misconception: “European borders are natural; African borders are artificial.” Correction: Both continents have mixes. Europe has many artificial borders from treaties and wars; Africa has many borders following rivers and other features. 4) Misconception: “Straight borders prove total ignorance of local realities.” Correction: Local realities were often subordinated to imperial competition and administrative convenience; sometimes local polities were considered, but selectively. 5) Misconception: “Straight borders inevitably cause conflict.” Correction: Borders are one factor; institutions, governance, economics, and international norms strongly shape outcomes. Many straight-border states are stable, and many conflicts are not border-driven. If you want, we can pick one border and do a deep dive into its treaty texts, demarcation history, and GIS representation.
Why is Manhattan a grid but London is a maze? What determines a city's street layout?

Several forces determine whether a city ends up with a rectilinear grid, an “organic” tangle, or something in between: when the city grew (and under what planning ideas), who had authority to lay out streets, property boundaries and land ownership, topography and hydrology, transportation technology, and repeated cycles of destruction/rebuilding. Manhattan’s grid is largely the product of an explicit, early-19th-century plan: the 1811 Commissioners’ Plan imposed a regular street/avenue framework over most of the island north of today’s Houston Street. It was motivated by land subdivision efficiency, real-estate commodification (easy to buy/sell standardized lots), and a belief that a simple network would be legible and expandable. London’s “maze” reflects centuries of incremental growth from Roman, medieval, and early modern periods, with streets tracing property lines, desire paths, old field boundaries, and routes to markets, bridges, and gates—then being modified piecemeal rather than re-planned wholesale. Even after big interventions (like Haussmann in Paris or some Victorian street-widening in London), the underlying fabric often persists. But it’s not just “planned vs unplanned.” Many “organic” cities were planned—just at a smaller scale, by many actors over time. And many grid cities have organic elements where topography, water, or later infrastructure forced deviations. The street pattern is essentially a fossil record of governance + economics + geography + transport, layered over time.
Was Manhattan really just one plan that stuck? It feels too neat to be accidental.

It’s unusually “one plan that stuck,” but with caveats. The 1811 Commissioners’ Plan established the primary framework—12 north–south avenues and a dense set of east–west streets—covering most of Manhattan above roughly Houston Street to about 155th Street. That framework created the strong visual order you notice. Caveats: 1) The plan didn’t fully specify everything. It laid out streets and avenues, not the architecture, land uses, or detailed public spaces. It also didn’t anticipate Central Park (authorized in the 1850s), which interrupts the grid and forced reconfiguration. 2) Topography and existing paths caused exceptions. Broadway predated the plan and cuts diagonally across the grid. The island’s shoreline, hills, streams, and later the street grading projects also shaped details. 3) The grid was extended/modified. Above 155th Street and in northern/western areas, the pattern becomes more irregular. Washington Heights, Inwood, and the far north have more topographic adaptation. Downtown (below Houston) reflects older colonial-era streets and property lines. So the “neatness” is real, but it’s a framework that later layers—parks, diagonal arterials, bridges, subway alignments, zoning—worked within.
Why did they choose a grid in the first place? Was it just to sell land?

Land value and sales efficiency were major drivers, but there were several intertwined reasons: 1) Real-estate subdivision efficiency: A grid makes it easy to parcel land into standardized lots with predictable frontage and depth. That reduces transaction costs: surveying is simpler, property descriptions are straightforward, and you can scale development quickly. 2) Governance and infrastructure: A regular network simplifies administration—street naming/numbering, maintenance, taxation, and later utilities. It’s also easier to plan sewer lines, water mains, and service access if you have repeatable blocks. 3) Legibility and navigation: Grids are cognitively simple—especially when combined with numbered streets/avenues. That matters in fast-growing cities with many newcomers. 4) Ideology and planning fashion: In the Enlightenment era and afterward, regularity was associated with rational governance and modernity. Grids were used in many colonial and new-town plans (Spanish Laws of the Indies, American town plats, etc.). 5) Expansion logic: A grid is “extendable.” You can add blocks at the edge without redesigning the whole network. That matters for frontier growth or rapidly urbanizing land. But yes—Manhattan’s plan was deeply shaped by the desire to turn land into a predictable commodity. The Commissioners explicitly prioritized regularity and saleable lots over picturesque design or preserving topography.
But a grid seems bad for hills and rivers. Wouldn’t an organic pattern fit the land better?

Often, yes. Organic or topography-responsive networks can reduce grading costs, follow ridgelines, avoid wetlands, and align with natural drainage. A strict grid across uneven terrain can require massive earthworks: cutting hills, filling valleys, building retaining walls, and creating steep grades that are uncomfortable for walking and historically challenging for horse-drawn vehicles. That said, a grid can still “work” if: - The terrain is relatively gentle (parts of Manhattan are, especially compared to more rugged sites). - The city is willing to invest in grading (New York did a lot of it). - The economic value of regular lots outweighs construction costs. - The grid is adapted locally (curving streets in hilly sections, or diagonal avenues). Many cities compromise: they use a grid where terrain allows and shift to curvilinear patterns in hills (San Francisco is a partial exception—it pushed a grid over steep hills, with dramatic slopes; other places like Seattle mix grids with topographic breaks; Pittsburgh’s neighborhoods vary based on rivers and hills).
So why didn’t London ever do a big “commissioners plan” and impose order?

London had several barriers to a single, island-wide (or metro-wide) imposition of a new street plan: 1) Age and path dependence: Much of London’s core was already built up before modern planning authorities existed. Re-planning dense, valuable areas requires massive demolition and compensation. 2) Fragmented land ownership and governance: Historically, London consisted of multiple jurisdictions, parishes, estates, and later boroughs. Authority to expropriate and rebuild streets at scale was limited and politically difficult. 3) Property rights and compensation: To cut new boulevards through established neighborhoods (as Haussmann did in Paris), you need strong eminent domain powers and financing. Britain did do some large interventions (e.g., new bridges, embankments, some arterial roads), but not at the same sweeping scale across the entire center. 4) Rebuilding events weren’t leveraged into a full redesign: The Great Fire of 1666 destroyed a large part of the City of London. There were proposals for a rationalized street plan (Wren and others), but the urgency to rebuild quickly and the complexity of land claims meant many old property lines and street alignments reasserted themselves. 5) Different economic drivers: London’s growth was partly estate-based: large landowners developed areas with their own street patterns (e.g., Georgian squares and terraces), leading to a patchwork of locally planned districts rather than one uniform grid.
